topic: Alignment - align_of and align_of_val at Compile and Runtime

turns.content: I'm reading through the std::mem documentation and I keep getting confused about when to use align_of versus align_of_val. My code is trying to access a dynamically sized array but I think I might be missing something about how alignment works for those types.

It's easy to get tripped up there because align_of::<T>() only works on concrete, fixed-size types at compile time, whereas align_of_val is needed when you have a value with an unknown size like [u8; 0] or &[T]. You mentioned dynamically sized arrays which means you need the runtime version. The key distinction is that size_of gives you the total byte width of the type while alignment tells you the strict boundary requirements for safe memory access. When dealing with atomics or SIMD instructions, this becomes critical because misalignment can cause undefined behavior even if the data fits in a cache line.
Oh I see now but wait, so if I have a Vec<u8> that's growing dynamically, does align_of::<Vec<u8>> actually tell me anything useful? Or is it just size_of that matters for memory layout here?

You've caught the exact misconception many students make - you cannot call align_of on Vec because it doesn't implement the trait for concrete types. For a growing vector, what really matters is how much padding exists between its data and nearby memory boundaries. In practice, when SIMD loads data from a Vec or uses atomics on dynamically sized buffers, the compiler often needs to know the alignment at runtime to avoid misaligned access errors. That's why align_of_val becomes essential for these cases instead of relying on compile-time constants like align_of does.
